prompt: Can you explain why enhanced Siri is delayed?
response: It’s extraordinarily hard, in the current security models, to make something like this (access to all of your data as input, as well as dangerous tool use) safe. I believe it requires an entirely new security model to do this well with any kind of scale.

prompt: What role does Reinforcement Learning play in LLMs?
response: Reinforcement Learning is remarkably effective at helping models “absorb” intuition in a particular speciality and get better and better at automatically doing well at tasks like what they trained on.

prompt: What challenges does Reinforcement Learning face in certain domains?
response: The problem is that RL takes an inexhaustible stream of ground-truthing, to constantly give the model feedback on what it should do to improve. In some domains, you can get that ground truth feedback easily: for example, playing chess or go. But lots of domains are hard to efficiently ground truth, for example they require doing an experiment in the physical world, or rely on human judgment.

prompt: What is RLAIF and how does it differ from RLHF?
response: Instead of Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) you get RL*AI*F... if you keep it to areas the LLMs are extremely good at (e.g. domains where the LLM is so over-qualified it rarely makes a mistake) you can use RLAIF to derive a new model that is wildly better than before on that particular speciality orders of magnitude faster than if you had to have humans in the loop.

prompt: What is an example of using RLAIF to improve LLMs for React code?
response: Generate React code to accomplish the user’s request for a simple UI. Then execute the code and poke at it with Playwright to get screenshots of it, and see how it responds to simulated user actions. Ask an LLM to judge the quality of the result. Does this look like what the user asked for? Does it appear to work as a user would expect? This gives an ability to get better and better at it by throwing a lot of compute at the problem.

prompt: What are the limitations of LLMs in writing code for frameworks like React?
response: LLMs do great at writing tedious code; they struggle with novel algorithmically hard code. Put another way, LLMs can use React well, but it would struggle to write React itself.

prompt: What happens if a model is overfit to a specific framework like React?
response: If I’m right that Anthropic has specially focused on React, I’d imagine the model got at least incrementally worse on non-React code. The model likely “pulls” more heavily towards React, and that means it gets confused more often for things code in other frameworks. Also note that this could mean the model won’t handle updates to frameworks well.

prompt: How do LLM improvements impact businesses that build adjacent tools or fine-tuning services?
response: A business to finetune models to specific use cases, or a carefully calibrated UX flow to help squeeze out higher quality React apps out of models... then a new model comes out that was directly trained on doing that use case well and it leapfrogs all of your linear improvements, obviating them. “Proprietary tools to squeeze slightly better quality out of a model” doesn’t seem like a great business.

prompt: Why does subtle quality improvement have a low ceiling in mass market products?
response: If your thing has a subtly better quality than the default option that can only be discerned by aficionados, you have a low ceiling; most people just won’t invest enough time to care.

prompt: Why might models trained on specific versions of frameworks struggle with updates?
response: Tailwind apparently came out with a new, incompatible version that differs from the one that Claude 3.7 was heavily RLAIF’d on. That means Claude 3.7 is actually quite hard to use with the latest Tailwind version. RL improvements in models are at a low pace layer; it might take a year or more for the model to catch up to how Tailwind is now written.

prompt: What makes LLMs powerful in terms of collective interestingness?
response: Ideas that people bring up in conversations are ones that they implicitly vote are interesting... This is one of the reasons that LLMs can be powerful; they’re trained primarily on the most distilled collective votes for interestingness across society.

prompt: How do LLMs change constraints on human-level reasoning?
response: LLMs are human-level reasoning at the speed of light, with infinite scalability. A lot of things that require human-level reasoning have always had a friction that is governed by the 'at the speed of a human' constraint... But LLMs change that force of gravity, and suddenly a lot of things that everyone has just known are obviously impossible surprisingly become possible.

prompt: How can LLMs be used as gap fillers in tasks?
response: LLMs are kind of like caulk. That is, they are gap fillers. Good enough to fill in the details for you once you give the imprecise high level structure.

prompt: What is the effect of LLMs on 'activation energy' for accomplishing tasks?
response: Being precise is hard--you have to think through real-world fine-grained detail, and it takes time. That's a high activation energy bar. But LLMs can do a lot of that for you; you say an intention, it helps do the detail work for you quickly and with reasonable quality. So the effective activation energy hump for humanity has gotten lower.

prompt: How do LLMs support flexible note-taking and communication?
response: With LLMs, you can write your notes in whatever form makes most sense to you. The LLM will be right there with you, understanding what you intend, adapt to you and be like 'I got it!'. Then the LLM can help you distill that higher-fidelity hyperobject into more specific dimensionally-reduced versions for specific other audiences.

prompt: How do ecosystems support mechanistic emergence for user needs?
response: A user’s use case is mechanistically met by the ecosystem if it can sift through everything that everyone has done before and find a good analogue to answer this user’s question. These require significant critical mass to get going. It only works for a given use case if either some subset of the users are willing to do quite a bit of work, which can then be used for the rest of the ecosystem, or someone else that came before the user already had almost exactly the same problem. LLMs lower this floor, because they are like a semantic lubricant.

prompt: What is the problem with permission prompts in current security models?
response: Permissions prompts are kind of like responsibility laundering. The system can't make a call itself so it asks the user a question they can't comprehend the implications of. The permission prompt boils down to 'do you trust this developer', where 'trust' is some ill-defined concept because it’s hard even for engineers to reason about what kinds of things saying 'yes' might actually cause to happen.

prompt: How could finer-grained security models empower users?
response: What if you could atomize security models down to a finer level of detail. Down to a point where users can make local decisions about local questions and also when it’s broken down to that level a lot of them are obvious. It would also allow expressing policies with nuance that are inexpressible today... In this central place a motivated user could affix rules of arbitrary nuance. With LLMs even more users have the necessary motivation to do the precise tasks.

prompt: What is the point of a security model?
response: The point of any security model is to allow users to accomplish their goals while keeping them out of trouble. The effectiveness of the security model is how much people trust it, with minimal configuration, to not accidentally embarrass them or expose them to harm.

prompt: What are the challenges of giving untrusted code access to email data?
response: Letting untrusted code see your email is terrifying. If it’s limited to a 1P service’s code that you allowed access to your email, it’s easier to trust. You just need to trust that one entity to have good security hygiene and not be incentivized to sell your data. But if it’s limited to code that 1P’s employees wrote, it falls into the tyranny of the marginal user; there is a low ceiling on the functionality that they can offer... To unlock the most value on your email you’d want an open ecosystem of 3P and LLM-written software. But that’s inherently dangerous! You’d need some kind of new security model to allow automated access of open-ended code from third parties while still being safe.

prompt: Why are some software products not built, even when technically feasible?
response: Sometimes it’s because it’s just not technically feasible. But more often it’s because it’s just not economically feasible. That is, it’s not worth the cost for someone to build and distribute it.

prompt: How does consensus relate to innovation in groups or LLMs?
response: Consensus always pulls towards mush. The centroid, the average. Notably, that centroid might not itself be a viable point... Consensus mechanisms don’t produce innovation. Innovation is surprising, outside the distribution, at the edge between consensus and chaos. Consensus mechanisms can only give innovative results if there’s a specific consistent bias in all of the components.

prompt: Why must prompters give LLMs a specific bias for innovation?
response: Models won’t push you to the edge. The edge of the distribution is where innovation happens. But models are consensus mechanisms that pull you towards the centroid by default. You, the prompter, must give them a particular direction, a consistent bias that allows it to innovate in a direction away from the centroid.

prompt: What does it mean for a project to become autoconverging or autocatalyzing?
response: Once a project becomes auto-converging, everything changes. Before that point, the project will fall apart if you remove the scaffolding... Once it gets to the point where it’s clearly working and valuable, it becomes auto-catalyzing. Past that point, the project has its own internal, auto-strengthening momentum. It becomes obvious what incremental work to do to make the project better at what it already does. At this point it’s free-standing, it’s alive. It can stand under its own weight and grow.

prompt: What are the challenges for teams going off-road together?
response: Part of the challenge going off road is there are no roads. So you have no default schelling point to cohere around as a team. Everyone pulls by default in a random direction, averaging to mush. That means that as a team going off-road together, you have to have a clear northstar vision you all believe is important and are sighting off of.

prompt: How does remixing drive creativity and innovation?
response: Everything everywhere for all time has been a remix. We build on our priors, things we heard and absorbed in the past, and extend them in ways we vote are interesting or innovative... The remix is composed of the underlying thing or things you built on, and the tweak you made. Conceptually the value of the tweak is tied to 'if you hadn’t done that, how long would it take for someone else in the swarm of the ecosystem to make effectively the same tweak?'... Note that it’s not just the tweak, but deciding to build on that particular combination of inputs, out of the universe of all possible inputs, that is the innovation.

prompt: Why is creativity intrinsically inefficient?
response: Creativity is intrinsically inefficient. Efficiency means 'doing the status quo more cheaply and reliably'. Creativity is variance outside the status quo, on the edge of chaos and consensus.

prompt: What does the process of 'chunking' mean for cognition and society?
response: The process we use to think is also a constant building on top of ideas that have been useful so far, with a little variation. We then keep the remixed things that turn out to be useful, and this compounds and builds on itself. This is the process of 'chunking' that allows significant and increasing leverage in the thoughts we can think. The same process happens in society and within our minds.

prompt: Why does every additional layer (ply) of thinking increase difficulty?
response: Every additional ply of thinking increases the difficulty by an order of magnitude. The uncertainty you have to navigate compounds at each ply. It gets harder to reason about. But it also gets harder to execute, because the likelihood you missed something in your analysis also compounds.

prompt: What distinguishes positive and negative product stickiness?
response: There are positive and negative flavors of what is called 'stickiness'. Positive: the user gets more value the more they use the product and so they don’t want to leave it. Negative: the user gets more and more stuck and can't leave the product. Sometimes the two sides are related; as in a user storing more data in a system and building their workflows around it. The more they store data, the more useful the tool gets for them… and also the more that it would be a pain to migrate everything out.

prompt: Why is software considered primarily social, not technical?
response: Software is primarily social, not technical. Like all social things, it must be grown, not built. Engineering is a social activity. Not individuals creating formally correct components separately that fit together. It's a co-creative co-ownership. Writing software together as a team is a social co-evolutionary process of generating understanding together.

prompt: What is a Schelling point, and why is it important for ecosystems?
response: The schelling point for an ecosystem is often just as important as the protocol. Git is the protocol; GitHub is the schelling point. The schelling point is where people go for discovery of new things. Without it, you don't know where the other good things that speak the protocol are. GitHub and Git both need each other.

prompt: What is an example of mismatched mental models in software usage?
response: They gave the tool to an accountant and watched how they used it. The user put in all of the numbers in the table… and then got out their pocket calculator to do the sums. They didn’t realize the superpower of computer spreadsheets. The mental model was 'paper spreadsheets, but in the computer.' But by being in the computer, the spreadsheet could be magical and interactive. A massively more powerful tool than the thing they superficially resembled.

prompt: How do infrastructure and quality projects differ over time?
response: Infrastructure projects: infinite time gives logarithmic returns. Quality projects: infinite time gives exponential returns. The difference between mediocristan or extremistan. The determining question is: is there a ceiling or not?

prompt: Why is it hard to gain trust in ecosystems if you aren't known?
response: It can be hard in an ecosystem to get others to trust you if they don’t know you. One approach is to go out of your way to make it extremely cheap for someone to detect if you cheat.

prompt: What is the significance of adding a staging environment for PMF?
response: You need to add staging at the point where you have users who will be mad at you if something breaks on main, because they rely on the tool. You have PMF for at least that one user!

prompt: What are the two main ways to get a team to move with coherence?
response: The first is to set a clear, compelling vision for everyone to sight off, that everyone is drawn towards. Another approach that works when you have a lot of internally motivated people who are not yet aligned is to set constraints. The constraints set a bias in the system; the random motion now has an asymmetry that pulls it in one direction.

prompt: Why can coordination be a costly and distracting activity for teams?
response: Coordination (within a team, or ecosystem) will take all the cost you're willing to give to it. An insatiable social vortex. 'I thought we'd be doing most of the R&D on the novel architectural stuff, but we're just spending all of our time debating which Vite config to use.' There's power in schelling points that everyone can agree are reasonable and good enough and stop spending time debating.

prompt: How does teamwork in ambiguous situations resemble a spiral rather than a circle?
response: As a team navigating ambiguity it can feel like you’re going in circles. But each time around to a place that looks superficially similar, you’re now individually and collectively wiser. Seen from above it looks like circles; from the side it looks like a spiral, making progress in a third dimension.

prompt: Why is there safety in numbers regarding breaking changes in ecosystems?
response: The likelihood of your workflow being broken by an upstream change in an ecosystem is tied to how many other people have a similar workflow and how loud they are. There's safety in numbers.

prompt: What causes software to 'rot' over time?
response: All software rots, even well-written software. The world changes, and so the software, fixed in time at the last time it was touched, no longer fits. A given piece of software can, all else equal, be better or worse at resisting rotting, but it can never fully resist rotting. The investment of maintenance energy counteracts rot.

prompt: How do programmers and LLMs differ in their approach to building things?
response: Building by addition and building by subtraction are fundamentally different. Building up from clay or chipping down from marble. Programmers tend to start with very specific, small things and then accumulate. LLMs tend to start with a very general idea and then carve down into specifics.

prompt: Why do WYSIWYG systems often have edge cases that are difficult for users?
response: The fundamental reason is because the view is a reduced-dimension visual projection of the underlying semantic model. In reducing dimensions, you must lose some of the nuance. It’s possible for there to be two visually equivalent view states that are different semantic states. That means when a user modifies the visual projection, the system sometimes has to make judgment calls about how to resolve the ambiguity in the underlying semantic model.

prompt: What is the danger of representing complex phenomena with oversimplified mental models?
response: When you have the wrong or ineffective mental model for a situation, it creates the possibility for a nasty surprise. The wrong mental model that doesn't actually capture the relevant dynamics of the system gives you a faux confidence... Every mental model is wrong. It must be. it’s projecting a multi-dimensional phenomena to a much smaller number of dimensions, which requires loss of information. Yet some mental models are more wrong than others.

prompt: How does knowledge grow according to the cycle of conjecture and criticism?
response: Knowledge grows from cycles of conjecture and criticism. That is, form a mental model, a hypothesis. Then expose that hypothesis to disconfirming evidence, for example ground truthing it. Guess and check. The disconfirming evidence gives you the information necessary to update your hypothesis to make it more accurate.

prompt: How do embeddings work in representing meaning?
response: Embeddings are fuzzy but precise. This allows them to be very good at capturing nuanced things that are hard to distill into formalized semantics.

prompt: What is the pattern for making decisions in ambiguity using rubrics?
response: A pattern I use to make decisions in ambiguity: abduct a rubric out of my head. Let’s say I need to choose between multiple options. I introspect and try to figure out the dimensions that matter the most to me. I then extract those dimensions into a column in a spreadsheet with either a continuous variable or a bucket (with a weight for each bucket). I then try to introspect about my intuitive weighting of those factors and abduct a ranking function across those factors. Then I put a bunch of items in, and see what ranking comes out. If the ranking 'feels' wrong I try to figure out what’s off. Perhaps a missing dimension. Perhaps the weighting of a factor is wrong. Then, I just keep doing this iteratively, putting in more options and tweaking the rubric until I have a final choice I feel good about. The rubric doesn’t tell me how to think, it helps me sharpen and engage with my intuition.

prompt: Why do people avoid learning about complexity, and what is the benefit of doing so?
response: People intuitively want to avoid learning about complexity because they fear it will slow them down. But it will actually speed you up to learn about gravity, instead of constantly running up against it. Now you can focus your time and effort on things that might work, not wasting time on things that definitely don't.

prompt: What is the Most Generous Interpretation (MGI) and why is it useful?
response: Try to use the MGI: Most Generous Interpretation. A good faith interpretation. Helps build bridges of understanding. If you don't use MGI, you're using others’ actions to help achieve your emotional goals and needs... Curiosity is the antidote. “Assuming this person is collaborative and competent, why did this bad outcome still happen?” You’ll assign less blame, allowing you to learn more.

prompt: How does building intellectually meaningful relationships lead to serendipity?
response: My personal serendipity engine works by cultivating intellectually meaningful relationships with people I run into who are interest*ing* and interest*ed*. But each conversation I have with an interesting and interested person, someone who wants to talk and explore ideas together, has the upside of possibly discovering a game-changing idea together, now or in the future. Capped downside, uncapped upside.

prompt: What is the 'apps as avocado slicers' frame?
response: I also love the essay’s "Apps as avocado slicers" frame.

prompt: What is prompt injection as a vulnerability in LLM systems?
response: We’re starting to see more awareness of prompt injection as a vulnerability.

prompt: What is MCP and why is it dangerous in the context of AI integration and browsers?
response: For example, this one uses MCP in ways that web publishers and others will dislike...It builds in MCP, (which bypasses same origin policy) directly into the browser (whose safe operation fundamentally *requires* the same origin policy!).

prompt: Why is the vertical integration of context and model into a hyper-aggregator dangerous?
response: That's one of the reasons why vertical integration of context+model into a hyper-aggregator is so dangerous!

prompt: Why do ranking systems almost never expose the “knobs” to users?
response:
Ranking systems almost never expose the "knobs" to users.

There are a number of reasons for this.

First, there’s the tyranny of the marginal user–if only 1% of users would have twiddled the knobs, then they’re not worth building because it might distract and annoy everyone else.

Second, they lock in whatever ranking ontology happens to be used right now.

That makes it harder to change internal ranking signals in ways that don’t fit within that exposed ontology.

Ranking systems often have fluid experimentation throughout all components that are hard to fit in a user-facing ontology.

If you change the ontology, how do you migrate users’ previous settings?

Third, you might reasonably think, “the user might set things now based on current low quality in some sub-domain, but if we improve that sub-domain, the user might have inadvertently boxed themselves out of a better experience.”

Fourth, exposing too many knobs might give insight into internal signals, making them easier for creators to game.

But finally and most importantly, consumer systems have an emergent gravitational pull towards engagement maxing.

A ranking system would never ship a toggle to “make me more bored.”

prompt: What is the center of gravity in the current AI era?
response:
The center of gravity in this era is not the models, it's the context.

There are a bunch of models of generally competitive quality and lightly differentiated abilities.

The model provider should not also own the context layer.

That's like the cell carrier trying to dictate you can only use their super-app on your phone.

prompt: What makes LLMs applied to ads powerful and potentially dangerous?
response:
LLMs applied to ads will be powerful and scary.

LLMs can do qualitative insight at quantitative scale.

When applied to "ads" on top of billions of users' intimate context, that analysis and manipulation could be so subtle no one knows it's happening.

That's an unprecedented amount of dystopian power.

Not just mass surveillance, not just the scale of mass media, but the power of precision targeted individual manipulation, all in one.

It’s impossible for any single entity to have that power without being corrupted by it, no matter how pure their intentions.

prompt: What are the three humanity-denying movements around AI described by the author?
response:
In the past I’ve enumerated three humanity-denying movements in the past around AI:

1) AI doomerism - the people who say AI is fundamentally an immoral technology and don’t want anyone to use it, despite its obvious power and potential to help humanity.

2) E/Acc - which includes people who might call you a speciesist for optimizing for human flourishing over an AI’s.

3) cynical engagement maximizers - the people who are happy to apply the social media engagement maximizing playbook to AI and make a quick buck.

prompt: Why does authentic collaboration become impossible with a party that is much more powerful than you?
response:
It's not possible to collaborate with something that's 10 times more powerful than you.

The power dynamic dominates the ability to collaborate authentically.

prompt: What is RLAIF and how does it affect generated code and design choices?
response:
What people like, but *more*.

The RLAIF loop runs without a human in the loop, using just the simulation of what the average human would like.

Turns out on average we don't like kino, we like slop.

Individually we like kino, but in different ways.

Together, as an undifferentiated mass, we like slop.

So slop is what the amplifying algorithms give us.

prompt: How do amplification algorithms shape behavior and habits?
response:
Amplification algorithms take the things you happened to do that one time and didn't dislike and makes them habits that you can't escape.

Or, amplify the things that people do on average.

Giving you something that is mid, engaging, addictive, mindless.

prompt: What does it mean that LLMs are not commoditizing?
response:
It’s not correct to say large language models are “commoditizing.”

Commoditizing means that each offering is effectively the same as others and there is no differentiation.

Intelligence is much less commodity than storage.

In intelligence there's more room for taste and differentiation.

The leading LLMs all have some distinctive differentiation and abilities.

Models aren't interchangeable, and there is a diversity of ones with different strengths... but there's no one provider that's obviously better than all the others.

Most importantly, there is a healthy and competitive race between a non-trivial number of powerful LLMs, which prevents any of the model providers from trying to flex their muscle too much.

prompt: What are the risks for companies trying to differentiate LLMs in niche verticals?
response:
Companies trying to differentiate an LLM model in their niche vertical risk general purpose models eclipsing them.

Only the big kids can compete in the general model race, because it’s so resource intensive.

If the general purpose model gets better enough, it could lap the capabilities of the vertical model.

Then the creator of the vertical model would find themselves on a tech island facing a dilemma.

1) Ditch their expensive proprietary investment and compete on the same turf as everyone else (but coming from behind since they’re coming from a different stack)

2) Double down on their proprietary edge, hoping to somehow surpass the big kids, but likely falling farther and farther behind.

prompt: Why do the models' rate of improvement matter to user power in API ecosystems?
response:
Do the models have more power, or the UI that builds on the models?

It comes down to "do users of an API have a BATNA or not", which is the case, even though the SOTA models keep on making leaps and bounds performance gains... they *all* keep making roughly those gains, which is important.

prompt: What are the implications of chatbots as omniscient main loops versus as contained features?
response:
Will the main loop of the AI-native experience be a chatbot that is omniscient and makes all of the calls?

Or will chat be a contained feature that pops up when necessary?

Everyone else assumes the former, but I assume the latter.

Chat is a feature, not a paradigm.

prompt: What is the connection between concentration of power (such as oil or platforms) and the ease of authoritarian control?
response:
If oil is found, it's naturally easier for a dictator to control, since it's a concentrated source of revenue. Just a small amount of mercenaries is enough. So there will be a series of dictators.

If there's no concentrated source of revenue, the people need to be involved and thus a more democratic path is likely.

With the internet and software, especially with platforms, you see this concentration of power effect. That then easily leads to certain kind of power dynamics. Ie just as a hypothetical example, if there's only a few closed conversation platforms, government can control them relatively easily.

prompt: How do cost structures relate to systemic change, especially with disruptive technologies like LLMs?
response:
Everything is held in delicate balance by cost structures.

When the cost structure changes discontinuously the system can be in a supercritical state.

Invisibly ready for a smashing new alignment that no player has discovered… yet.

But when they do, the whole world changes in an instant.

The changes can be terrible for large swathes of society… or great.

LLMs, like other disruptive general purpose technologies like the printing press, electricity, and the internet, fundamentally change cost structures.

prompt: What is the difference between value and values?
response:
Value is about what the machine thinks of you.

A legible quantity that is instrumentalist and finite and fundamentally about your relation to the machine.

Values are about your aspirations, your infinities, about *you* as an intentional human being.

prompt: What does 'coactive substrates powered by LLMs will be like 3D autocomplete' mean?
response:
Coactive substrates powered by LLMs will be like 3D autocomplete.

Imagine a system that understood your context deeply and was totally aligned with your interests.

Like code completion… but for things you find meaningful in your life.

The autocomplete metaphor is nice because it shows you the system can do anything you can do, no difference.

prompt: What is the Lego analogy for Malleable Software?
response:
Lego is composable and decomposable,

It allows for a huge number of creations and variations.

But most people won’t build big and complex things with it - there is freedom but also a lack of planning and ambition

Kid lego sets come along with instruction manuals and just the right pieces to build incredible things ... which kids do ... but then they leave them as static finished outputs rather than playing/composing/decomposing with them.

prompt: What is the recipe versus well-stocked kitchen analogy for creativity and malleable tools?
response:
Having a well-stocked kitchen is great in terms of creativity and freedom to create.

But without a good recipe, most people will end up making pretty ordinary and predictable meals.

You can use a well crafted recipe to reach a higher peak, but often your ability to follow a particular recipe to get to a higher peak is different from your ability to create such a higher peak recipe yourself.

When do we want the flexibility of the well stocked kitchen?

And when do we want the recipe that shows us how to create something that is above our usual ability?

And when can we learn from these recipes so that we can then tweak them, making raspberry pie instead of the recipe blueberry pie, or vegetarian tacos instead of meat ones?

prompt: Why won't malleable software have mass consumer adoption until the same origin model is superseded?
response:
I don’t think malleable software will have mass consumer adoption until the same origin model is superseded.

Users would rather have their data in the experience than a malleable UI, if they had to choose.

The same origin data friction is just too big a headwind to overcome, which forces users to make that choice.

prompt: What is the difference between infinite orchestration and infinite centralization in the same origin model?
response:
In the same origin model with infinite software you get either infinite orchestration or infinite centralization.

Infinite orchestration: the users spending all of their time orchestrating their data across a foam of little micro app bubbles.

All rind, no pulp.

Infinite centralization: a single app to rule them all.

An app that knows us better than ourselves.

Not just for you, but for everyone.

An unimaginable amount of centralized power.

The former is unlikely to be viable, which leaves us with the latter by default.

So we need to change the same origin model.

prompt: What does it mean for software to orbit around data, not code?
response:
I want software that orbits around data, not code.

What software should be... tools that mold themselves to you.

Not ‘user’ centered, human centered.

prompt: What is the current problem with the app model's security and trust assumptions?
response:
For the last thirty years, we've had to trust the creators of the code.

When policies are attached to data, and everything else is permitted, you don’t have to trust the creators of the code.

prompt: Why is attaching policies to data more secure and less trust-dependent than the current app model?
response:
Imagine a security model that allowed you to not have to trust code, even if it works on sensitive data.

That would be a small tweak with massive implications.

prompt: Why is the requirement for open-ended trust in the app model a problem?
response:
Turing complete code below a policy is open-ended.

That's what makes the same origin model hard to reason about and trust.

It attaches implied policies to the app/origin, but allows that app to execute arbitrary turing-complete code on your sensitive data and have network access.

One of the reasons you see permission prompts is that there's turing complete stuff the origin could see, so you need a "do you trust the owner of this origin in an open-ended way.”

That makes it hard to make a call, and you just have to trust the creator of the code.

You have to trust anything the creator of the code could do now or in the future.

The requirement for open-ended trust closes off the possibility.

When you attach policies to data, you could allow anything that the policies allow, an open-ended possibility.

prompt: Why are data-attached policies more precise than code-attached ones?
response:
Turing complete things are open-ended, so policies on them must be looser, more generic.

Policies on data can be tighter, more precise.

That’s because they don’t need to handle the open-endedness of code inside themselves.

They only need to define what open-ended things can do.

prompt: What would it mean to have a runtime that enforces the legality of computation graphs according to data policies?
response:
Imagine a runtime whose job was verifying that a given graph of computation was legal according to the policies attached to the data it was operating on.

It wouldn’t have any business logic of its own.

That would come entirely from the policies on the data.

There’d be no legitimate reason to modify the runtime itself.

prompt: Why did the Cloud win so comprehensively, despite loss of control/agency?
response:
Why did the Cloud win so comprehensively?

After all, it has some downsides, like giving up control and agency over our data.

But it has other benefits.

One is that it’s really convenient to have a beefy computer, sys admin’d by a professional, that will almost always be up 24/7.

But I’d argue the more valuable skill is the ability to aggregate signals from a crowd and distill them into useful information.

The wisdom of the crowd can’t be distilled if all of the individual points of insights aren’t combined *somewhere*.

prompt: Why shouldn't I trust your browser (or installed extensions) even if I trust mine?
response:
I trust my browser and you trust your browser.

But I shouldn't trust *your* browser.

The extensions you install are implicitly trusted by you.

Assuming you did it knowingly and understand the implications, which obviously is not a good assumption in practice.

But I shouldn’t trust the extensions you installed.

You implicitly trust the software you choose to install, but that doesn’t mean you should implicitly trust the software other people chose to install.

The root of the trust is the (hopefully informed) *decision*.

prompt: What are the stages of the S-curve and how do they affect company behavior and values?
response:
The upward part of an S-curve is a wild and wonderful place to be.

“We’re geniuses! And we can have massive success without having to compromise our values.”

But of course it’s less that you’re a genius and more that you’re on the upswing of an S-curve.

if your company is riding a powerful S-curve, almost no matter how much you mess it up, it still keeps growing.

Then at some point, you pass the inflection point to the top part of the S-curve.

The S-curve still has momentum, but in a way that increasingly erodes your soul.

Once the S-curve tapers, it was time to turn up the knob on extraction, since explosive organic growth is now behind you.

If you need to squeeze out growth and the underlying organic growth has slowed, to keep up growth you need to squeeze on extraction more.

As you turn up the knob on extraction, it becomes increasingly aligned against your users, and your own values.

prompt: What are the problems with referring to people as 'users' or 'consumers' in tech?
response:
I find the term “users” that is common in the tech industry to not be a great term.

It feels like talking about people who are addicted to a drug.

"users" and "consumers" are not about humanity, it's about the slice of the human that is commercial activity.

So much of our activity is now implicitly or explicitly commercial, so it's dominated our humanity.

If you had to pick one term for humanity, these words are kind of gross.

Instrumental, not sacred.

Finite not infinite.

What if we stopped being consumers, primarily?

What if we were people?

Or humans?

What about members?

Emphasizes a collective ownership and destiny over the service they’re taking part in.

An active choice to be a part of the experience.

prompt: What is the relationship between tool capability and sense of belonging?
response: If you think about it, this is almost a tautology: most things that make you more capable, necessarily make the capabilities of someone else less useful to you. Feels like the inverse of Robert Wright's *Nonzero* thesis.

prompt: How does the relationship between language and reasoning compare to that between math and physics?
response:
The relationship between reasoning and language is similar to the relationship between physics and math.

When a supergiant star goes supernova, the universe doesn’t do math to determine if it should end up forming a black hole or a neutron star; an unfathomably large number of complex interactions at the subatomic level dictates the outcome.

However, math is a surprisingly good substitute, to the point that (despite never having physically observed the process in real time), we can be confident that if the remnants of the supernova are below a precise threshold (~3x greater than the mass of the sun), a neutron star will form.

So, an LLM doesn’t reason, but manipulating language is a very good substitute for reasoning.

If we’re going to build “strong” AI a la Searle it will need to work in a different medium.

prompt: How do LLMs change the approach of data science and industrial scaling of insight?
response:
A data science approach is an industrial process.

It assumes generic, industrial scale insight, not situated or personal.

Before you had to do that to scale.

But now LLMs give you qualitative insights at quantitative scale.

In the past you had to reduce the data down to its common denominator to do math on it.

Losing the nuance.

Now you don't have to.

prompt: Why did social media become so problematic when combined with mobile phones?
response:
The thing that made social media problematic was social combined with mobile phones.

Individually either would not have been *that* bad.

We have our phones without arms’ reach nearly every waking minute, so the infinite feed is always within reach to colonize every free second.

Also now the social network is with you everywhere, if you’re sharing content it has you preening for optimized pictures to share with your followers.

Instead of experiencing the thing, how to perform for the others that you're experiencing the thing.

prompt: Why do dysfunctional organizations persist and become worse over time?
response:
People who can stand a dysfunctional environment are more likely to survive it.

That then makes the environment more distilled and dysfunctional along that dimension.

A runaway effect until the organization can no longer produce enough value in the world to stay alive.

prompt: How does perception of inevitability influence the power of entities and people's willingness to resist?
response:
A powerful entity that acts like it’s inevitable, and that everyone believes is inevitable, will *be* inevitable.

People decide whether to push back against a powerful entity based on their beliefs of how powerful it is.

If they believe that sticking their neck out has downside and no upside, they won’t do it.

The Saruman understands the power of memetics in this age of infinite content.

prompt: What is the difference between assuming the best and worst in people with respect to modern politics?
response:
In modern times the most important political dimension is “do you assume the best in people or the worst?”

Do you assume by default that everyone is strong and capable or do you assume they are lazy and incompetent?

This is the difference between the Sarumans and the Radagasts.

prompt: Why must boundaries emerge in complex adaptive systems, and what form should they take?
response:
Boundaries must emerge in every complex adaptive system.

Without them you'd get background cacophony that drowns out everything but the average.

Don't try to fight the formation of boundaries.

Just make sure they are nested, fractal, fluid.

prompt: What is Blink?
response: Blink, for those of you who don't know, is a new rendering engine originally based on WebKit that we're using in Chromium. The mission statement of Blink is to improve the open web through technical innovation and good citizenship.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google I_O 2013 - Fireside Chat with the Blink Team.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: What was the purpose of creating Blink as a separate project from WebKit?
response: When we made the decision to create Blink, it was a really hard one that we had to come to terms with. But it came down to fundamentally that Chromium used a different multiprocess architecture from other WebKit-based browsers. And that increased complexity not just for the Chromium team, but for everybody who was using WebKit. So we mentioned back when we made the announcement that we anticipated we could remove about 4.5 million lines from the code base.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google I_O 2013 - Fireside Chat with the Blink Team.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: What is the Oilpan project?
response: So one of those is a project called Oilpan. And this is just, again, an experiment into seeing if we can move the DOM nodes into a garbage-collected heap. So today, the DOM nodes, they use a reference counting system, and it interacts in a very complex way with the JavaScript engine. And so if this works, it could really help make that much simpler and much easier to understand and less error-prone.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google I_O 2013 - Fireside Chat with the Blink Team.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: What is lazy block layout?
response: Another cool one is called lazy block layout. Again, a very early experiment. The idea is, what if we could make very large but simple layouts much, much faster to do? So if you're only using a handful of features, why can't we make that much, much faster? And if it works, this would allow things like infinite scrolling lists to be much easier to accomplish on the web platform.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google I_O 2013 - Fireside Chat with the Blink Team.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: What is the launch review process for new features in Blink?
response: The next thing I want to talk about is our transparent launch review process. Launch review process is healthy for projects in many different ways. For us, it's important for external contributors to understand how they can work with our team to land their code in the code base. It's also important to build consensus early on in the development process and avoid conflicts later. And finally, and this one is really important for us, we don't want to ship proprietary features. And the launch process helps avoid doing that. Basically, when we ship something in Chrome on the web platform, we want all browsers to ship it, not just Chrome.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google I_O 2013 - Fireside Chat with the Blink Team.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: How does the intent to implement and intent to ship process work in Blink?
response: So the way this works in practice is, say you're a Blink engineer and you want to add a web platform feature or somehow change the behavior we expose to web developers. Before you start writing code, we ask you to first send an intent to implement email to our public mailing list, BlinkDev. This email follows a template format. We ask you to provide a link to the specification, a description of use cases and motivation, and finally, give an overview of compatibility risk. And in the last section, what we're looking for is, do other browser vendors think this feature's a good idea? Is it making progress through the standardization process? And usually there's some discussion that follows about whether this is something that belongs in Blink or not. Later, when the feature's ready to ship, we ask them to send another email. And intent to ship email, which is scrutinized a little bit more carefully.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google I_O 2013 - Fireside Chat with the Blink Team.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: What is chromestatus.com?
response: So in addition to BlinkDev, we have a cool site called chromestatus.com, which I'll show you guys now. Basically, this is a spreadsheet...where each row corresponds to a feature that we're currently working on or recently shifted. There's some logistical information like the feature's name, who the owner is, the bugs, its implementation status in Chromium. And then over here, the cells are colored. And these columns correspond to things that we think reflect web citizenship. So for example, progress through standardization, the views of other browser vendors, the excitement from web developers. And our goal with this is to have a nice visual representation of how each feature reflects our web citizenship. And then as you scroll down, you can kind of see the project's overall web citizenship just based on these colors. So right now, this is just an iframed Google spreadsheet, but we're hoping to make progress here.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google I_O 2013 - Fireside Chat with the Blink Team.m4a_spaced.txt

Otherwise, it just becomes: 'You convince all your users, all the users everywhere, to flip their experimental flags on.'And now that's de facto. And so we are definitely trying to figure out how to balance this. So you want people to be able to use the features, to use them in real-life websites, and give feedback to standardization for other browser vendors. But you also don't want to get them stuck with sort of a bad implementation that you can't change.

prompt: How does the Chrome team use counters to help decide when to deprecate features?
response: And this is important for us as the Chrome, as the Blink team, is that we can get information on when we can responsibly deprecate features. And so a good example of this is with prefixed versus unprefixed features. Oftentimes, when we release an unprefixed version of a feature, we can't remove support for the prefixed version because the web would break. And so we're stuck with this old code path for the prefix version when we really don't need it anymore because we have the unprefixed version. And so we can use this system to track the not only adoption of new features, but also decline of old features, and no one can feel comfortable removing them.

response: I think feature detection is the right way to handle this. prompt: What is multi-threaded style recalc and layout?
response: Multi-threaded style recalc and layout. It's going to be awesome. So, one thing, I'll tell you a little bit of background. And in a lot of cases, there's a lot of time spent in layout, sometimes in style recalc, depending on how much heavy DOM manipulation you're doing. And this is pretty much single-threaded from going from style recalc out to seeing something on the screen. And it can slow down your app. So being able to basically take some of these operations and throw them to a separate thread or a separate core, I'm totally jazzed about those experiments.

prompt: What are out-of-process iframes, and why are they important?
response: That to me is one of the dreams I've always had for Chrome's multiprocess architecture, that it could not just sandbox tabs, but actually sandbox origins. And to me, it's just really exciting. So, the idea of turning Blink into more of a frame server, if you will, like in the iframe sense, and allowing us to isolate origins to their own processes, I think it's just going to be fabulous, especially in this cloud world, this world where most of your data is in the cloud. The sandbox that we have in Chrome is fabulous at allowing users to be protected against malicious web content that might try to exploit a bug to persist malware on your computer, it's great at that, protecting against that. But a lot of the valuable data that users have is right there in the cloud. And our sandbox doesn't do anything to help protect that data. But if we were able to isolate origins into their own processes, then, man, that would be a huge step forward for web security.

prompt: What are Web Components?
response: Web Components is this new set of technologies that we looked at, but we created by looking at these potholes and decided to fill them naturally as things that fit inside of the web platform as a whole. Rather than building a whole new crazy thing on the side, which all of us have done before, we decided we're going to fill those holes naturally into something that feels like just part of the web platform, just part of the standard things that you normally do in the browser, not a script and not some crazy invention that is completely brand new. And so, what we did is we kind of found out that there's three pieces that are missing. And these pieces are Shadow DOM, the custom elements, and the templates.

response: Templates allow you to define HTML DOM snippets very easily. So today, you're doing this in long bits of string and script, or you're doing script tags with some templates there. This basically just allows you to define it directly in your HTML, have it sort of frozen off to the side so you can swap it in when you need it.

prompt: What is the purpose of the Shadow DOM?
response: Shadow DOM is about encapsulation and providing you with the ability to add these extra chunks of tree on this chunk of DOM tree and render them as if they're part of the bigger rendering.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google I_O 2012 - The Web Platform's Cutting Edge.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: How does render time composition work with Shadow DOM?
response: This is a really, really cool concept right here. It's a render time composition. You don't have to move those nodes around. They're still there. If you walk your first child, next child, next sibling, there's going to be only the green tree inside of it. But at render time, the Shadow DOM tree will be placed on top and rendered as if it's there.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Google I_O 2012 - The Web Platform's Cutting Edge.m4a_spaced.txt

prompt: What are insertion points in Shadow DOM?
response: We have this really, really cool concept called insertion point. Insertion points are special elements that you can put into your Shadow DOM subtree to designate where the children of the Shadow root will poke through. And so as you can see there, I have two insertion points. And when it's rendering, again, at render time only, those children will shine through inside of the Shadow DOM tree. So in a way, it allows you to have this way to compose the Shadow DOM subtree around your document node.

prompt: What are CSS variables?
response: CSS variables is one new technology. The spec is nearing completion. We actually have it implemented in Chrome behind a flag. CSS variables allow ... you basically, just to define it once. So they're actually really, really powerful. You can do all kinds of cool stuff with cascade and referencing other variables.

prompt: What is TodoMVC?
response: If you don't know what TodoMVC is, it's just a reference application. It's something that people use to write this application again in their framework to compare it and see how it feels compared to other frameworks.

prompt: What is the Shadow DOM?
response: Shadow DOM is a pillar of Web Components. This brings encapsulation to the platform for the first time, which is really, really, really big. And we'll show that to you in a little bit. It's also incredibly complicated, especially to grasp right away. But the big point is it gives you the ability to encapsulate things within a tag.

prompt: What is a polyfill?
response: A polyfill is a JavaScript library that basically mocks out a future standard so that you can use it before it actually is deployed in all modern browsers. The goal with polyfills is to have really high fidelity. So, that way, later, you don't even need it.

prompt: What are mutation observers?
response: mutation observers allow for the efficient observations of DOM, so when new nodes are added or attributes are changed. This one's actually already shipping in most modern browsers.

prompt: What are pointer events?
response: Pointer Events. This is a specification that unifies the event models for touch and mouse; it allows you to write the same code and have it run on mobile devices and desktop devices as well.

prompt: What are custom elements?
response: custom elements. This is another pillar of Web Components. It allows you to define your own tags and have them operate just like any other tags in the browser. It allows you to create these elements and put whatever you want inside of them, even ones of arbitrary complexity, and then have them treated just like any other tag that you're familiar with, like the video tag or Canvas or whatever.

prompt: What are HTML imports?
response: HTML imports... allows you to package CSS, JavaScript, HTML into a single module and import it into your app. And it allows reusability across multiple applications or multiple parts of your application.

prompt: What is MDV (Model Driven Views)?
response: this next one is MDV, which stands for Model Driven Views. This is the only one in this list that isn't really an emerging standard yet, although hopefully one day it will be. It's something that allows you to do data binding directly in HTML. And as we've been playing with all these, this is one that really allows us to eliminate a lot of boilerplate code in practice.

prompt: What are Web Animations?
response: Web Animations unifies CSS and SVG animations and exposes the primitives beneath those. And it allows you to create really complex animation sequences separate from the things that you want to animate.

prompt: What does 'everything is an element' mean in this context?
response: So the idea is every single thing is a module, right? And you use an element. And you build applications out of bigger and bigger elements made of more and more elements, up to and including an actual app. By doing this, and you've got all these reusable little portions, you keep a developer's context really local. You reduce bugs. And it makes it so that from the perspective of a developer at any given time, an application can be arbitrarily simple and complex at the same time. So we wanted a system that weaves together the platform to enable component-based apps. So everything is an element, and all elements have Shadow DOM encapsulating them.

prompt: How are configuration and output handled in custom elements in Polymer?
response: Just like every other DOM element, the way you pass in configuration is via attributes, and the way you pass things out of it is via events. So from the outside perspective, it's just an element.

prompt: What is the significance of the declarative event mapping feature in Polymer custom elements?
response: one of the first big features that we add is declarative mapping of custom DOM events to methods within the element. So in this case, when a select event is fired, the panel select handler element with that method name, this is not code that's executed, will be rerun on the actual element.

prompt: What is the purpose of data binding powered by MDV in Polymer?
response: one of the big things we add is data binding, and this is powered by MDV. This radically reduces code complexity.

prompt: What is the benefit of being able to encapsulate existing functionality into a single tag using custom elements?
response: you can make your own custom elements. And one of the other uses for that is you can wrap up existing functionality on the web and put it in a single tag, and it's encapsulated from the rest of your page, so you don't have any conflicts or system-level dependencies that you're creating.

prompt: What is the interoperability significance of Web Components with respect to different libraries or frameworks?
response: What's cool about this Web Components approach where you have actual encapsulation between them is it allows for the first time the potential for components from different sets to intermingle seamlessly. So, in this world, interoperability layer is the DOM, and everyone knows how to use the DOM, how to pass an attribute to receive events out. And, again, the Polyfill, the Polymer library as well is layered. So you can take just the parts you want.

prompt: What are evergreen browsers?
response: We call these the evergreen browsers. These are browsers that are modern, that they support many of the new standards. They also, importantly, keep their users updated by default. So users don't have to do anything special just to stay on the newest one. So the users aren't going to live in the past for long, because when there's a new version, they'll just come along to the new version, which is really important.

prompt: What is the late stage of a paradigm?
response: The late stage of a paradigm is extremely boring. All of the requisite variety in the system has been slurped up into the small number of hyper gravity wells. There's little movement in the system except in the hyper gravity wells. Those gravity wells move slowly because they're so large, so swaps of position happen less often. All the remaining energy goes into adding ornamentation and differentiation into increasingly small details and niches, so much so that at the 100,000 foot view you can't see any changes at all. Like a universe approaching its heat death. Everything slows to a crawl. Because *everything* slows, on a relative basis you don’t notice anything getting slower, but you *feel* like you’re trapped in amber. Stagnation.

prompt: What is the 'same-origin paradigm' in computing?
response: The same-origin paradigm is the laws of physics that both periods of this paradigm have in common; the animating logic for this whole current universe. The same origin model has been so prevalent for 30 years that we literally can't imagine anything else. It colors all of our assumptions about privacy models (and thus the horizon of viable experiences) for everything.

prompt: How does reasoning work in society, and how is it related to LLMs?
response: The vast majority of 'reasoning' is actually a fuzzy interpolation of previously cached answers. The caching is not just in a single brain, but in the collective actions and records of all of society. At some point, a single brain fires up its expensive System Two to squeeze out a new seed crystal of novel reasoning. That seed crystal is released into the world: put into practice, maybe written down. As other people come across it, they make a judgment call: is it useful? ... The LLM is now a hyper-dimensional crystal that captures a whole universe of useful bits of cached bits of reasoning. The LLM can only do a facsimile of reasoning, but that is also all that humans do the vast, vast majority of the time.

prompt: What is the structure of language according to this text?
response: Language isn't just a large mush of words, it's a high-dimensional fractally structured crystal. It's a tool applied to the real world, so the structure it absorbs into itself is a reflection of actual structure in the real world, because if it weren't then the swarm wouldn't bother to absorb it. It's only if lots of members of the swarm find it useful that the information becomes a new wrinkle in the hyperdimensional structure of language that is then propagated forward in time and space. If no one uses a new wrinkle, it fades away over time, entropy eroding it.

prompt: Why is programming hard?
response: One reason that programming is hard is the arcane and unforgiving syntax. If you mess up, the compiler will yell at you with cryptic errors. Luckily, LLMs are very good at interpreting those cryptic errors and fixing the problem… even with no humans in the loop. But programming is also hard because programming requires generalizing and generalizing is hard. Generalizing requires finding disconfirming evidence proactively and changing a thing to be resilient to it. Constantly figuring out how your thing will break and defending against it. Proactively finding disconfirming evidence and incorporating it into your thing is terrifying and hard.

prompt: What is a 'system of record' and why is it important in B2B software?
response: For any given task, users can’t have more than one system of record, it constantly collapses to a stable state of a single system of record. The entity that is the system of record is the center of that universe; the point around which everything else orbits. It is a discontinuously more sticky position to be in than alternatives. An extraordinarily juicy prize that the best B2B companies have earned. If you’re the system of record, you’ve won. If you aren’t a system of record, you have only a tenuous grasp and are likely to lose it at any time. A binary outcome of winners and losers. Systems of record are a kind of gravity well. Gravity wells are hyper centralization dynamics...

prompt: What is confidential computing?
response: Confidential computing is, basically, “secure enclaves in the cloud.” Most chips being deployed today in production clouds support confidential computing modes. They can run a VM, fully encrypted in memory, with a minor (1–2%) performance overhead. Without confidential computing, an SRE at one of the cloud providers could theoretically peek into the memory of your running VM. Your VM in the cloud is your turf… but the hosting authority can still peek inside. With confidential computing, it’s your turf, and the hosting authority can’t peek inside. Think of it like an embassy. Your own sovereign territory, embedded in another context... This guarantee is not absolute (just like the host country could theoretically forcibly enter an embassy), but it is a boundary orders of magnitude stronger than the status quo of “Keep out. Or Enter. I’m a sign, not a cop.”

prompt: What is remote attestation in the context of confidential computing?
response: Confidential computing also allows another magic trick: remote attestation. It’s possible for a service to remotely verify that another remote service is running a particular VM with a particular SHA. For example, an unmodified open source library. If you trust that library to have certain behavior, you can then trust that node to have that behavior, even if you don’t know or trust *who’s* running the node. You can build a trusted fabric out of untrusted nodes, because you can verify what each node can do, remotely. This allows assembling a heterogenous common fabric with a consistent set of laws of physics within it. A planet-spanning common fabric with different laws of physics.

prompt: What is a user-first model and how is it different from origin-first or local-first models?
response: We should aim for a user-first model. Not origin first. That prioritizes the domain, and the ease of implementation. It leads to accelerated hyper-aggregation. Not local first. An improvement over origin-first, but not enough. It still by default prioritizes the domain by default. The combinatorial possibility of software is still not possible by default. Instead: User-first. Operating on the user’s turf under their full control. This can be local… or, with confidential compute, remote. Not sharded by domain; one file system for experiences to coordinate in. More challenging to implement, but unlocks a universe of combinatorial potential.

prompt: How do chaos monkeys strengthen systems?
response: Chaos monkeys generate swarms of disconfirming evidence for a system. Systems that survive a chaos monkey get stronger. Too much chaos could kill the system, knock it out of the game. So start with small amounts of chaos and ramp up the chaos as you go and as it survives longer.

prompt: How do LLMs resemble a photocopier, and why does that matter?
response: LLMs are like a magical photocopier. They can do a surprisingly good copy of things they've never seen before. But each thing they generate is slightly degraded. If you run it through the system 10x times it will be unintelligible. Humans in the loop are what re-ground it, inject fresh, non-degraded reasoning into the system.

prompt: Why are big ideas with a full incremental glide path valuable?
response: Big ideas with a full incremental glide path are radically more valuable. Most big ideas don’t work. Somewhere along the path there’s a hidden trap, some unforeseen game-over event. The longer the path, the more likely there's at least one step that has a non-obviously non-viable step somewhere in it that is game over. An idea with a full incremental glide path is different. A full path from where you are to a great outcome, where each step is continuous with the one before it, and no miracles are required. Many miracles are hidden; it’s easy to ignore them ... But what matters is not whether you recognize it requires a miracle, but that it does ... That’s why it’s important to seek disconfirming evidence, including by seeking novel perspectives, to discover hidden miracles along the path.

prompt: What does it mean for a system to be efficient but brittle?
response: Each row, each process is like a Lilliputian rope holding Gulliver in place. The organization becomes “efficient” but brittle, unable to absorb the real world disconfirming data.

prompt: What is the difference between complication and complexity?
response:
The difficulty comes in two distinct flavors of complication and complexity, intermixed in different proportions. And those two different flavors are fundamentally different.

Imagine the problem space as a labyrinth that you must navigate to arrive at your goal. Complication can be thought of as how many hazards there are and how much the paths twist and branch. Crucially, though, the walls are well defined: at every point it’s clear which way to turn to avoid the walls, or how many different paths you have to choose from at this branch point, or when you’re about to step into a hazard.

Complexity is the degree to which the labyrinth is shrouded in a deep, perhaps impenetrable fog. Where the walls are, where the branch points are, and where the hazards are is no longer clear until you are right upon them. The general direction of the goal is your only beacon for you to rely on. Unit for unit, complexity is significantly more difficult than complication.

prompt: How does complication affect how you should approach a problem?
response: Complication rewards velocity, organization, and execution. The path is clear, so you can move quickly down it without fear of going off road. Keeping track of the general direction of the goal will help you tend to take the fork that is more likely to get you closer. There’s nothing particularly difficult to reason about, so putting your head down and focusing on “turning the crank” is sufficient to make forward progress.

prompt: How does complexity affect how you should approach a problem?
response: Complexity rewards judgement, thoughtfulness, and reason. Any given step could have you collide with a wall or fall into a pit, so it’s important to carefully consider each move. Take each step tentatively, and as you think you can make out shapes in the fog — perhaps a hazard is just ahead — be willing to pause and reconsider your path. Velocity in impenetrable fog in unknown parts of the labyrinth would be foolhardy.

prompt: How can you determine what approach is appropriate for a given problem based on complication and complexity?
response:
For any given problem it’s important to understand the mix of complication and complexity so you can pick the correct mode of action.

If you’re in a non-complicated and non-complex problem — a wide open room with no fog — point toward the goal, strap a rocket on, and light it!
If you’re in a complicated but non-complex space, carefully considering and re-considering each step before you take it would lead to moving unconscionably slowly. Focus on execution above all else.
If you’re in a non-complicated but complex environment — something more like a foggy wide-open field — then you may be able to move more quickly than you could in a more complicated environment, since you know you’ll encounter fewer obstacles.
If you’re in a complicated and complex environment you should move as carefully as possible.
Of course, situations are rarely at one of these extremes, but rather some mix.

prompt: How can complexity be reduced over time?
response:
Complexity arises primarily due to the novelty of a problem space. As the novelty of the labyrinth fades the complexity will diminish.

For example, when you return to parts of the labyrinth you’ve already visited, you’ll be more likely to remember the positions of the hazards, walls, and branches, giving you the confidence to move more quickly. In effect the fog will have lifted somewhat.

And it’s not just learning from parts of the labyrinth that you’ve already visited. To the extent that you’ve already explored a similar labyrinth and can generalize your learnings, or that you have a guide who is familiar with this labyrinth, or that it is a labyrinth that is well-traveled by others, the fog will not be as dense.

prompt: Is complexity a result of failing to define a goal or make assumptions?
response: Complexity is a frustrating thing to to deal with, and it can be tempting to view it as a failure to define a goal crisply enough, or to make simplifying assumptions aggressively enough. But complexity is an unavoidable fact in novel problem spaces. The worst thing you can do is pretend it doesn’t exist.

prompt: What is the difference between a zero-sum game and a positive-sum game?
response: In a zero sum game, the proper stance is competitive. But in a positive sum game, the proper stance is collaborative. If the two parties together come to a better understanding than either had individually before, everyone wins.

prompt: What is the collaborative approach to debate?
response: The collaborative approach to debate is one of the best ways to understand complicated, and especially complex, problem spaces. The result is a stronger idea that more perfectly anticipates the messy reality it will have to operate within.

prompt: What is the importance of the openness of the web?
response: One of the things that really got me into the web was like, view source. I was able to go and see, so wow, this is a thing on the web, view source. Wow, that's how it's been put together... it's great for tinkerers because you don't need to get anyone's permission. You don't need to buy anything. You can just use a text editor that comes on your computer and get into it that way. That's totally how I started. That's how I bet a lot of us started on it.

prompt: What is a performance budget in web development?
response: That's one of the things I'm hearing sort of becoming more and more popular and it's music to my ears is the idea of having a performance budget as part of your design process. And that's a big part of Rails.

prompt: Why is the web fast by default?
response: the web is fast by default, right? I mean, if you have a blank page, pretty fast. It's all regression.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Chat with Alex Komoroske (Live from Google I_O 2015) - Coffee with a Googler.mp4_spaced.txt

prompt: How do push notifications work on the web and what are the security considerations?
response: the way that it works is: you visit a site. And what we encourage developers to do is, in context, explain to users why they might want push notifications. So, say for example, you visit the Guardian. You've read multiple articles on a given topic. And they say, hey, at the end, you like this topic. Would you like to be notified when there's more articles on this topic? Well, yes. Yes, I would. You tap that on. And then, of course, Chrome verifies, hey, Guardian would love to send you notifications, just to make sure that you knew what you were doing. And at that point, if you say yes, they'll allow them to register for notifications. There's an important bit, too, that says, then say you register. And it says, this sounds good. You register. And they send you a notification every minute. We also make it very easy in each notification we show. There's a prominent 'Site settings' button. If you tap that, it's just a couple more taps to turn off notifications for that domain to make it very easy for you to revoke that permission if you decide that that site wasn't using it in a respectful way.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Chat with Alex Komoroske (Live from Google I_O 2015) - Coffee with a Googler.mp4_spaced.txt

prompt: What is background sync and why can it be challenging to implement on the web?
response: background sync is a functionality that native apps often have. They can sort of wake up in the background, refresh in their resources. Why don't we just bring this to the web? Well, it's actually really hard. So imagine that you visit a site. Every day on your personal computer, it's an embarrassing celebrity gossip site that you don't want to admit to anybody you visit. You go off and you never would visit that at work because you're supposed to be working. So you visit it and we say, okay, cool. You visit this pretty often. Let's let your background sync that. Well, now you go to your work network.and it syncs in the background, and your boss goes, Jake, what are you doing? Why are you wasting time on the company network? So that's just, we have to think very carefully about these things and make sure that users have the right expectations about what's happening so the right things can happen and they don't inadvertently waste their battery or leak personal information or what have you.
source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/corpus/transcript-Chat with Alex Komoroske (Live from Google I_O 2015) - Coffee with a Googler.mp4_spaced.txt

prompt: What does it mean for an idea to be a "gilded turd"?
response: In the extreme, something that looks superficially great but turns out to be terrible as you look closer is a gilded turd.

prompt: What does it mean for an idea to be a "sublime jumble"?
response: They look superficially messy, random, rough and ready. But the closer you look, the more convincing they become. A sublime jumble. You see that the fundamentals are even stronger than they looked at the beginning. As you dig deeper you have more and more “whoa!” moments, and get even more convinced by the idea.

prompt: What is "minimum viable decentralization"?
response: The right approach is to have minimum viable decentralization to start, and then grow from there.

prompt: What are the three components needed to meet the decentralizable bar?
response: Publish immutable data; Use universal identifiers; Sign everything with user-controlled keys.

prompt: What is a meta-product?
response: Its quality and usefulness isn’t just a reflection of the effort invested by its builders. The quality and usefulness is also in the quality of the ecosystem it reflects. Even in the early days of Search, if Google engineers stopped working on it, it would still increase in quality as the quality of the web grew. This is the power of meta-products: products that complement an ecosystem. Meta-products can evolve and be open-ended. They reward users experimenting and trying new things. The product itself co-evolves with the emergent uses in the wild.

prompt: What is the cold start problem in apps?
response: The magnitude of the cold start problem is to some degree an artifact of the current laws of physics of apps. 1) Making an app is hard. 2) Distributing it to the right user at the right time is expensive. 3) When users start with a new app, they start from nothing. 4) Users have to distrust new experiences until they earn their trust, because the experiences can do anything with data they have access to. 5) The only viable business model for consumers is ads.
